FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


March 11, 2005

John G. Barisone, City Attorney
City of Santa Cruz
333 Church Street
Santa Cruz, CA 95060

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-271

Dear Mr. Barisone:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Councilmember Tony
Madrigal for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

A. Decisions Regarding Councilmember Madrigal's Employer

1. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during
which the city council is providing direction to its labor negotiators concerning a new
MOU between the city and Service Employees International Union 415 ("SEIU")?

a) If he may not participate in the decision, what are his duties when the item is
considered in open session?

b) If the item is heard in closed session, may the council member remain in the
room when it is being considered?

c) May he review notes and minutes recorded by council members and city staff
and negotiators during the closed session?

2. May he participate in decisions concerning the layoff of SEIU employees?

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 2

B. Segmentation Questions

3. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during which the city council is providing direction to its labor negotiators concerning a new MOU between the city and Operating Engineers Local No. 3 ("Operating Engineers"), or subsequent closed sessions concerning implementation, application or interpretation of that new MOU?

4. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council decisions concerning the elimination of vacant Operating Engineers bargaining unit positions or in decisions concerning the layoff of Operating Engineers employees?

5. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions in which the council provides direction to the city manager concerning adjustments of department head salaries?

6. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council budgetary decisions?

C. Exceptions

7. If Councilmember Madrigal is disqualified from participating in the above decisions and deliberations, may he appear before the city council personally, or as part of a public demonstration to urge or petition the city council to take a particular action or make a particular decision relative to the matters under deliberation and discussion by the council?

8. May Councilmember Madrigal attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the SEIU bargaining team at which city/SEIU MOU negotiations will be discussed? May he attend meetings at which the union members will vote to adopt or reject a proposed SEIU/Union MOU?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Mr. Madrigal will have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from participating in city council sessions concerning a new MOU between the city and SEIU, unless these decisions will have no financial effect at all upon SEIU. The prohibition would apply to both open and closed sessions.

a. Councilmember Madrigal must comply with steps outlined in regulation 18702.5(a)-(d). In an open session meeting, Councilmember Madrigal must publicly identify the type of economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest, and then he must recuse himself and leave the room. There are special rules for closed session meetings outlined in regulation 18702.5(c).

b. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifying conflict of interest, therefore he cannot remain in the room. See discussion below.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 3

c. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifying conflict of interest which
bars him from reviewing non-public information.

2. Because SEIU would be indirectly involved in these decisions,
Councilmember Madrigal's ability to participate in these decisions would depend on their
financial impact on SEIU's gross annual receipts. We are not the finder of fact, thus we
must leave this determination to you and the council member pursuant to regulation
18705.3(b)(2).

3, 4 and 5. City Council decisions regarding MOU negotiations between the City
and Operating Engineers, elimination of vacant Operating Engineers positions, layoffs of
Operating Engineers members and adjustments of department head salary are decisions
that may be interlinked with decisions in which SEIU, Councilmember Madrigal's
employer, is directly involved. Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of
interest is "significantly interrelated" to another decision, the official will be required to
disqualify from both decisions. Because we are not the finder of fact, we cannot
determine whether these decisions are inextricably interrelated.

6. Councilmember Madrigal may participate in a final vote to adopt or reject the
agency's budget pursuant to regulation 18709(c). However, without more specific facts
we cannot determine whether Mr. Madrigal may participate in decisions regarding
individual components of the budget. See the segmentation discussion below.

7. Councilmember Madrigal may not appear before the city council personally to
urge the city council to take a particular action or make a particular decision unless he is
solely representing his own personal interests. See discussion below.

8. Councilmember Madrigal may attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the
SEIU bargaining team as long as he is not making, participating in making or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision.

FACTS

On November 2, 2004, Tony Madrigal was elected to the Santa Cruz City Council
and he took his oath of office on November 23, 2004. Mr. Madrigal has been gainfully
employed by Service Employees International Union 415 ("SEIU") since April 2001.

Currently, SEIU represents approximately 490 city employees, or about 60% of
the city's entire workforce. Another labor union, Operating Engineers Local No. 3,
represents approximately 157 city employees or 19% of the city's workforce. Mr.
Madrigal has previously represented city employees on behalf of SEIU; however, he does
not currently represent city employees, but other Santa Cruz County public and private
employee bargaining units which are also represented by SEIU.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 4

According to the SEIU website, SEIU members pay union dues in the amount of
1.255% of their gross base pay. In the calendar year 2004, minimum dues per month per
member earning over $43 per month were $18 and dues were capped at $100 per month.
As of January 1, 2005, SEIU members will continue to pay union dues at the rate of
1.255% gross base pay, the minimum monthly dues payment will be $20 and there will
be no maximum cap. You state that SEIU is a source of income to Mr. Madrigal and
union dues constitute a source of revenue to SEIU.

The current memorandum of understanding ("MOU") between SEIU and the city
delineates terms and conditions of employment, including compensation and benefits for
the city's SEIU employees. The MOU was recently approved by the city council and
SEIU, but will be reopened in 2005 to renegotiate compensation and benefits. In light of
this fact, the city and SEIU will be negotiating the terms and conditions for an SEIU/city
MOU during Mr. Madrigal's term in office. The city will be simultaneously conducting
similar MOU negotiations with its Operating Engineers bargaining units. In accordance
with the Brown Act, Government Code section 54957.6, the city council, in connection
with these negotiations, will periodically meet with its appointed labor negotiators in
closed session to provide negotiation direction. The council will likely continue to do so
not only through the end of those negotiations, but periodically thereafter in connection
with the implementation, application and interpretation of those MOUs.

Although the city negotiates separate MOUs in connection with its employees
represented by Operating Engineers, many of the provisions of the Operating Engineers
MOU possibly mirror their corresponding provisions in the SEIU MOU, and a number
of the provisions in both the SEIU and Operating Engineers MOU are legally required to
be identical. An example of the latter are terms and conditions relating to the city's
retirement plan which, under the applicable Public Employees Retirement System
regulations and statutes, must be uniform for the bulk of the city's workforce whether
represented by SEIU or Operating Engineers. The city also provides a uniform benefits
package to all employees without reference to a given employee's bargaining unit. As a
result, all bargaining units effectively negotiate for the same medical, dental, vision, life
insurance and long term disability benefits. In addition, the SEIU and Operating
Engineers' negotiations are interrelated by "salary compaction" issues whereby the city,
as employer, is asked to consider maintaining an across-the-board salary scale that
differentiates, by percentages or dollar amount, the amount of salary a
manager/supervisor (Operating Engineer) can expect to receive in comparison to a city
"line employee" (SEIU).

In fiscal year 2004, 60.3% of the city's operating capital, including both funds
allocated to the city's general fund and funds allocated to the city's various enterprise
funds (e.g. the refuse fund, the water fund and the sewer fund), was used to pay employee
salaries and benefits including the salary and benefits payable to SEIU employees,
Operating Engineer employees, and the city's department heads who are not represented
by a union. Of this, 74.3% of the city's general fund operating expenditures were
attributable to city employee salaries and benefits, 29.5% of all city operating funds,
including general and enterprise operating funds, were dedicated to SEIU employee

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 5

compensation and benefits, while 46.47% of general fund operating expenditures were
attributable to SEIU employee compensation and benefits.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of interest Prohibition

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step
analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

1. Is Councilmember Madrigal a "public official"?

The conflict of interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section
87100.) As a council member in the City of Santa Cruz, Tony Madrigal is a "member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency" and is, therefore, a
public official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).)

2. Will Councilmember Madrigal be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

The conflict of interest prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating
in making, or attempting to use one's official position to influence a governmental
decision. (Section 87100.) A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the
official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, determines not to act
because of a conflict or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (See regulation 18702.1.) By deliberating, voting, committing his agency to a
course of action, or entering into any contractual agreement on behalf of the city council,
Mr. Madrigal will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.

A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; regulation

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 6

18702.2.) By participating in city council discussions and negotiations, or by advising,
making recommendations to the council on an issue, Mr. Madrigal would be making or
participating in making a governmental decision.

Regulation 18702.3 provides that an official is "attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer."

For example, absent an exception, Councilmember Madrigal would be attempting
to use his official position to influence council decisions by appearing before the city
council to urge the council to take a particular action or make a particular decision on
matters related to his economic interests.

In contrast, so long as he is not making, participating in making or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a government decision as set forth
above, Councilmember Madrigal may attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the
SEIU bargaining team at which city/SEIU MOU negotiations will be discussed or at
which the union members will vote to adopt or reject a proposed SEIU/Union MOU.²

Exception:

The only possible exceptions that may apply are found in regulation
18702.4(b)(1)(A-C). Even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may appear
before his or her agency as a member of the general public in the course of its prescribed
governmental function in order to represent himself or herself, but only in regard to
matters related solely to his or her personal interests, including, but not be limited to:

"(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members
of his or her immediate family.
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole
direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse
jointly exercise sole direction and control." (Ibid.)

Under the facts that you provide, it is unlikely that this provision applies because
Mr. Madrigal does not appear to be solely representing his own personal interests.

_______________
² Elected officials do not give up their First Amendment rights to speak out on public issues upon being
elected to office. (City of Fairfield v. Superior Court of Solano County (1975) 14 Cal.3d. 768, 780-782).
In general, there is nothing in the Act that would prohibit Councilmember Madrigal from speaking out as a
private citizen from the audience on matters related to his personal interests (Regulation 18702.4(b)(1)(A-
C)) or from speaking to the press or public.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 7

Recusal Requirements: If a public official is enumerated in section 87200
(including city council members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion
of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of
the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

Since Mr. Madrigal is a member of the city council, a position enumerated in
section 87200, these requirements apply to him.

3. What are Councilmember Madrigal's economic interests — the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Your letter states that Councilmember Madrigal is "gainfully employed" by
SEIU, which is not a business entity. Assuming that he has received at least $500 within
the past 12 months, he has an economic interest in SEIU as a source of income. (Section
87103(c).)

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 8

Councilmember Madrigal also has an economic interest in his personal finances
and those of his immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will
have an economic interest if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.)

Since you have not provided facts suggesting that this economic interest is
implicated, we do not further discuss this basis for disqualification.

4. Are Councilman Madrigal's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?

A person, including a source of income, is directly involved in a governmental
decision if the person initiates a proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or
similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1)-a(2).) A
person is the subject of a proceeding concerning the decision before the agency if the
"decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with," that person. (Regulation
18704.1(a)(2).) If a person who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly involved.

SEIU DIRECTLY INVOLVED

Renegotiations of MOU between the city and SEIU:

SEIU is a named party in the renegotiations of the MOU between the city and
SEIU. Furthermore, these memoranda represent contractual commitments between the
city and SEIU. Thus, SEIU is directly involved in the governmental decisions of the city
council concerning these renegotiations.

SEIU INDIRECTLY INVOLVED

Renegotiations of MOU – Operating Engineers:

SEIU is not a named party in negotiations of the MOU between the city and
Operating Engineers.

Eliminating Vacant Positions:

We assume that eliminating vacant employment positions is a matter initiated by
the city council and not the unions (SEIU and/or Operating Engineers). Thus, the unions
are not the subject of the proceedings in which these decisions will be made and they
would be indirectly involved in this type of decision.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 9

Layoffs of SEIU employees:

The local's appearance in matters involving employee layoffs presumably could
be in a representative capacity rather than being a named party. When acting in a
representative capacity, we have advised that a person (which in this instance would
include the local) is indirectly involved in the governmental decisions concerning that
matter. (Orlik Advice Letter, No. I-98-175; Doyle Advice Letter, No. A-97-071; Nelson
Advice Letter, No. I-91-443.)

5. Applicable Materiality Standard

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.

Directly Involved Nonprofit Entities:

When a nonprofit entity that is a source of income to a public official is directly
involved in a governmental decision, should that decision have any financial effect at all,
even a penny, on that entity, it is deemed to be material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).) This
would apply with respect to the SEIU MOU.

Indirectly Involved Nonprofit Entities:³

In all the other decisions you have described, SEIU is indirectly involved.
Regulation 18705.3(b)(2) lists the materiality standards for source of income, nonprofit
organizations that are indirectly involved in a decision. The thresholds the regulation
vary depending upon the gross annual receipts of the nonprofit organization. We cannot
determine which materiality standard in the regulation is relevant to your facts because
we do not know SEIU's gross annual receipts. As discussed below, however, this step
may not be necessary to complete the analysis.

"Nexus Rule":

There is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in cases where
there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source of income and to the official's public
agency. The "nexus" rule is only relevant when a decision indirectly affects an economic
interest of the official. Under the "nexus rule,"

"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a
source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public

³ Note that if either the "nexus rule" applies, or if the decision is "inextricably linked" to the SEIU decision,
the stricter "one-penny rule" would apply to the decision. (See discussion below.)

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 10

official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
(Regulation 18705.3(c), emphasis added.)

The rationale for the "nexus" test is that a public official may not accomplish in
his or her public capacity what the public official is paid to accomplish in his or her
private capacity. When a connection exists between a person's job and his or her role as
a public official, there is a presumption that the value of the employee's services to the
employer is based on the fact that the employee is a public official. Thus, the materiality
threshold is understandably much lower.

In this case, we would look to see when Mr. Madrigal is acting in his official
capacity as a council member, whether he is accomplishing the purpose for which he was
hired by SEIU. However, this is a fact-sensitive question and would need additional facts
concerning his employment with SEIU to make a determination.

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon Councilmember Madrigal's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? (Does a conflict of interest result?)

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Renegotiation of MOU – SEIU:

The dues paid by members of SEIU are income to the union which is determined
according to a formula based on the percentage of members' gross base pay. Any
renegotiation of the bargaining agreements that affects the amount of gross wages paid by
the city to SEIU members will have an effect upon the income received by the union.
Since SEIU is directly involved in these renegotiations and is subject to the "one penny"
rule under regulation 18705.3(a), it is reasonably foreseeable under these facts that the
renegotiation of the bargaining agreements will have a material financial effect on the
union. For this reason, Mr. Madrigal has a conflict of interest prohibiting his
involvement in the city council's renegotiations with SEIU, unless an exception applies.

Renegotiation of MOU – Operating Engineers:

Although the city operates under a separate MOU in connection with Operating
Engineers, many of the provisions of the Operating Engineers MOU are legally required
to be identical to provisions of the SEIU MOU. In effect, negotiations involving certain
provisions of the Operating Engineers MOU would result in identical benefits for SEIU
members. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable under the facts you provide that
renegotiations of the Operating Engineers MOU would have a financial effect on SEIU.
If the effect is material, Councilman Madrigal would have a conflict of interest.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 11

prohibiting his involvement in the city council's renegotiations with Operating Engineers,
unless an exception applies.

Eliminating Vacant Positions:

Assuming that elimination of vacant positions is a matter initiated by the city
council and not the unions, SEIU would be indirectly involved. Thus it would be subject
to thresholds in regulation 18705.3(b)(2) which vary depending upon the gross annual
receipts of a nonprofit organization. Because we do not have specific information about
the financial impact of these decisions or know SEIU's gross annual receipts, we cannot
determine if the financial effect of the governmental decision will meet the applicable
materiality standard.

7. and 8. The "Public Generally" Exception and Legally Required Participation.

The conflict of interest which is the focus of your question derives from the
financial effect of renegotiations and other decisions involving a source of income to
Councilmember Madragl. For the "public generally" exception to apply, a significant
segment of the public would have to be affected by the decisions in substantially the
same manner as the source of income. (Regulation 18707(b).) Since a significant
segment would not receive the same financial benefits that SEIU will receive under renegotiation of the MOU with the city council, the "public generally" exception does not apply.

Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
governmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. The rule does not apply when there is an alternative source of decision-
making consistent with the statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708.) Thus, it
only applies when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made without the
participation of the disqualified official. Since you have not provided facts relating to the
application of this exception, we cannot advise you regarding the application.

Segmentation:

Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably
interrelated" to another decision, the official will be required to disqualify from both
decisions. Regulation 18709(b) states that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated'
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the
result of another decision."

You suggest that several of the decisions in question which only indirectly
involve SEIU may be "inextricably interrelated" to the decisions in which SEIU is
directly involved. Each of the governmental decisions should be analyzed independently
to determine whether there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on the
public official's economic interests. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) This is
ultimately a factual determination we cannot make.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 12

If the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" to the SEIU MOU decision, Councilmember Madrigal may be able to participate in some of these decisions. If the decisions are "segmented" into a series of decisions, Mr. Madrigal may participate in those decisions that do not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on his economic interests.

Segmentation may apply as follows:

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following conditions apply:

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the other decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any way; and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified."

Several of your questions related to segmentation.

1. Operating Engineers' contracts, adjustment of department head salaries, and elimination of vacant city employment positions:

Applying this regulation to these decisions, the city would first have to make a decision concerning the SEIU contract without the council member's participation, regarding both the terms of the contract and elimination of vacant SEIU positions. Second, these decisions would have to be final and no subsequent decision would affect them. Under such circumstances, it appears that the council member may be able to vote on the Operating Engineers' contracts, the vacant operating engineer positions and department head salaries. However, whether regulation requirements can be met is a factual question. Because we do not have specific facts, it is not possible to determine if a particular decision is subject to segmentation.

File No. A-04-271
Page No. 13

2. Budgetary decisions impacting employee salaries and benefits:

Your question concerns budgetary decisions that have an impact on the amount of city funds available for city operations, including employee salaries and benefits.

You have indicated that in fiscal year 2004, 29.5 percent of all city operating funds, including general and enterprise operating funds, were dedicated to SEIU employee compensation and benefits, while 46.7 percent of General Fund operating expenditures were attributable to SEIU employee compensation and benefits.

Thus if the amount of general and enterprise funds increase, so would the amount available to fund SEIU compensation and benefits. If the amount of general or enterprise funds decrease, the funds available for SEIU salaries and benefits would also decrease. However, an increase or decrease in these funds does not necessarily mean a corresponding increase or decrease in SEIU compensation and benefits. There appears to be some relation, but without more specific facts, it is not possible to determine if a particular decision is subject to segmentation. In any event, pursuant to regulation 18709(c), Councilmember Madrigal may participate in a final vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget.⁴

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\04-271

⁴ Regulation 18709(c) states that "[o]nce all the separate decisions related to a budget or general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget or to adopt, reject, or amend the general plan."

c) May he review notes and minutes recorded by council members and city staff

2. May he participate in decisions conceming the layoff of SEIU employees?

B. Sesmentation Ouestions
3. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during
which the city council is providing direction to its labornegotiators conceming a new
MOU between the city and Operating Engineers Local No. 3 ("Operating Engineers,), or
subsequent closed sessions concerning implementation, application or interpretation of

the elimination of vacant Operating Engineers bargaining unit positions or in decisions
concerning the layoff of Operating Engineers employees?
5. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions in which the
council provides direction to the city manager concerning adjustments of deparknent
head salaries?
6. May Councilmernber Madrigal participate in city council budgetary decisions?
C. Exceptions
7. If Councilmernber Madrigal is disqualified from participating in the above
decisions and deliberations, may he appear before the city council personally, or as part
of a public dernonstration to urge or petition the city council to take a particular action or
make a particular decision relative to the matters under deliberation ana discussion by the
council?
8. May Councilmember Madrigal attend meetings of the SEIU membership or
the SEIU barguningteam at which citylSEIU MOU negotiations will be discusseaZ Uuy
he attend meetings at which the union members will vote to adopt or reject a proposed
SEIU/Union MOU?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Mr. Madrigal will have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him from
participating in city council sessions conceming a new MOU between the city and SEIU,
unless these decisions will have no financial effect at all upon SEru. The prohibition
would apply to both open and closed sessions.
a. Councilmember Madrigal must comply with steps outlined in regulation
18702.5(a)-(d). In an open session meeting, Councilmember Madrigal must publicly
identify the tlpe of economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest, *d th",
he must recuse himself and leave the room. There are special rules for closed session
meetings outlined in regulation 18702.5(c).
b. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifuing conflict of interest,
therefore he cannot rernain in the room. See discussion below.
c. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifying conflict of interest which
bars him from reviewing non-public information.
2. Because SEIU would be indirectly involved in these decisions,
Councilmember Madrigal's ability to participate in these decisions would depend on their
financial impact on SEIU's gross annual receipts. We me not the finder of fact, thus we
must leave this determination to you and the council mernber pursuant to regulation
t87os.3(b)Q).
3,4 and,5. City Council decisions regarding MOU negotiations between the City
and Operating Engineers, elimination of vacant Operating Engineers positions, layoffs of
Operating Engineers members and adiustments of departrnent head salaries are decisions
thatmay be interlinked with decisions in which SEId, Councilmember Madrigal,s
employer, is directly involved. Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of
interest is "inextricably interrelated" to another decision, the official will be required to
disqualifu from both decisions. Because we are not the finder of fact, we cannot
determine whether thi:se decisions are inextricably interrelated.
6. Councilmember Madrigal may participate in afinal vote to adopt or reject the
agency's budget pursuant to regulation 18709(c). However, without more specific facts
we cannot determine whether Mr. Madri galmay participate in decisions regarding
individual components of the budget. See the segmentation discussion below.
7. Councilmember Madrigal maynot appear before the city council personally to
urge the city council to take a particular action or make aparticular decision unless he is
solely representing his own personal interests. See discussion below.
8. Councilmember Madrigal may attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the
SEru bargaining team as long as he is not making, participating in making or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a goverrmental decision.
sections.facts:
On November 2,2004, Tony Madrigal was elected to the Santa Crvz City Council
and he took his oath of office on November 23,20A4. Mr. Madrigal has been gainfully
employed by Service Employees Intemational Union 415 (,.SEIU;,; rir"" April 2001.
Currently, SEru represents approximately 490 city employees, or about 600/o of
the city's entire workforce. Another laborunion, Operating Engineers Local No. 3,
represents approximately 157 city employees or lg% otthi city's workforce. Mr.
Madrigal has previously represented city employees on behalf of SEIU; however, he does
not currently represent city employees, but other Santa Cruz County public and private
employee bargaining units which are also represented by SEIU.


File No. A-04-27t
According to the SEIU website, SEIU members pay union dues in the amount of
1.255% of their gross base pay. In the calendar year 2004, minimum dues per month per
member earning over $433 per month were $18 and dues were capped at $i0O pe. morth.
As of Januaty 1,2005, SEIU members will continue to pay union dues at the rate of
1.255% gross base pay, the minimum monthly dues payment will be $20 and there will
be no maximum cap. You state that SEru is a source of income to Mr. Madrigal and
union dues constitute a source of revenue to SEIU.
The current memorandum ofunderstanding (*MOU') between SEIU and the city
delineates terms and conditions of anployment, including compensation and benefits foi
the city's SEIU employees. The MOU was recently approved by the city council and
SEIU, but will be reopened in 2005 to renegotiate compensation and benefits. In light of
this fact, the city and SEIU will be negotiating the terms and conditions for an SEIU/city
MOU during Mr. Madrigal's term in office. The city will be simultaneously conducting
similar MOU negotiations with its Operating Engineers bargaining units. In accordance
with the Brown Act, Government Code section 54957.6,the city council, in connection
with these negotiations, will periodicallymeet with its appointed labornegotiators in
closed session to provide negotiation direction. The council will likely "orrtirrr" to do so
not only through the end of those negotiations, but periodically thereafter in connection
with the implementation, application and interpretation of those Mou,s.
Although the city negotiates separate MOUs in connection with its employees
represented by Operating Engineers, ffiffiy of the provisions of the Operating Engineers
MOU precisely mirror their corresponding provisions in the SEru MOU, *d u number
of the provisions in both the SEIU and Operating Engineers MOU are legally required to
be identical. An example of the latter are terms and conditions relating to tfrl city,s
retirement plan which, under the applicable Public Employees Retirement System
regulations and statutes, must be uniform for the bulk of the city's workforce whether
represented by SEIU or Operating Engineers. The city also provides a uniform benefits
package to all employees without reference to a given anployee's bargaining unit- As a
result, all bargaining units effectively negotiate for the same medical, dental, vision, life
insurance and long term disabilitybenefits. In addition, the SEIU and Operating
Engineers' negotiations are interrelated by "salary compaction" issues wLereby the city,
as employer, is asked to consider maintaining an across-the-board salary scale that
differentiates, by percentages or dollar amount, the amount of salary a
manager/supervisor (Operating Engineer) can expect to receive in comparison to a city
"line employee" (SEIU).
In fiscal year 2004,60.3% of the city's operating capital, including both funds
allocated to the city's general fund and funds allocated to the city's various ente,rprise
funds (i.e. the refuse fund, the water fund and the sewer fund), was used to pay employee
salaries and benefits including the salary and benefits payable to SEIU r*pioy"".,
Operating Engineer employees, and the city's department heads who are ntt represented
by a union. Of this, 74.3% of the city's general fund operating expenditures were
attributable to city employee salaries and benefits,29.5%oof all city operating funds,
including general and enterprise operating funds, were dedicated to SEIU "*ploy""
compensation and benefits, while 46.47% of general fund operating expenditures were
attributable to SEIU ernployee compensation and benefits.
sections.analysis:
Conflict of interest Prohibition
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." (section g1001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in agovemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step
analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a
grven governmental decision. (Regulation I 8700(bxl)-(8).)
1. fs Councilmember Madrigal a 6.public officialrr?
The conflict of interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section
87100.) As a council member in the City of Santa Cruz,Tony Madrigal is a "member,
officer, ernployee or consultant of a state or local government agenct'; and is, therefore, a
public official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the eci. lSection 8204g;
regulation 18701(a).)
2. will councilmember Madrigal be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict of interest prohibition covers specific conduct: making participating
in making, or attempting to use one's official position to influence a govemmental
decision. (Section 87100.) A public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the
official, acting within the authority of his or her office orposition, votes on a matter,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, determines not to act
because of a conflict or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (See regulation 18702.1.) By deliberating, voting, committing his agency to a
course of action, or entering into any contractual agreement on behalf of the;iry council,
Mr. Madrigal will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.
A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recofiImendations to the
decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; regulation
18702.2.) By participating in city council discussions and negotiations, or by advising,
making recornmendations to the council on an issue, Mr. Madrigal would be making or
participating in making a goverrmental decision.
Regulation 187023 provides that an official is "attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, afiy member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Atternpts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer."
For example, absent an exception, Councilmember Madrigal would be attempting
to use his official position to influence council decisions by appearing before the city
council to urge the council to take a particular action or make a particular decision on
matters related to his economic interests.
In contrast, so long as he is not making,participating in making or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a govemment decision as selforth
above, Councilmember Madrigal may attend meetings of the SEru membership or the
SEru bargaining team at which citylSEIU MOU negotiations will be discussed or at
which the union mernbers will vote to adopt or reject a proposed SEIU/Union MOU.2
Exception:
The onlypossible exceptions that may apply are found in regulation
18702.4(b)(1XA-C). Even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may appear
before his or her agency as a member of the general public in the course of its prescribed
govemmental function in order to represent himself or hersel{, but only in regard to
matters related solely to his or her personal interests, including, but not be limited to:
"(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the
official or mernbers of his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members
of his or her immediate family.
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole
direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse
jointly exercise sole direction and control.,, (Ibid.)
Under the facts that you provide, it is unlikely that this provision applies because
Mr. Madrigal does not appear to be solely representing his own personal interests.
2 Elected officials do not give up their First Amendment rights to speak out on public issues upon being
elected to office. (City of Fairfield v. Superior Court of Solano County (1975) 14 Cal. 3d. rci,nO-lt:21.
In general, there is nothing in the Act that would prohibit Councilmember Madrigal from speaking out as a
private citizen from the audience on matters related to his personal interests (Regulation f AlOZ.+1U11t1e-
C)) or from speaking to the press or public.
Recusal Requirements: If a public official is enumerated in section 8720O
(including city council mernbers) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion
of the item, orally identiff each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5@), on the record of
the meetingl' Q) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion anilor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
regulation 18702.\ subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
Since Mr. Madrigal is a member of the city council, a position enumerated in
section 87200, these requirements apply to him.
3. What are Councilmember Madrigal's economic interests - 
the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on thepublic generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section s7 I 03(d); regulation 1 8703. I (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 187 03.3);
a
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(section 87 103 ; regulation I 8703.5).
Your letter states that councilmember Madrigal is "gainfully employed,, by
SEru, which is not a business entity. Assuming that he has received at least $500 within
the past 12 months, he has an economic interest in SEIU as a source of income. (Section
87103(c).)
Councilmember Madrigal also has an economic interest in his personal finances
and those ofhis immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will .o.rtt in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.)
Since you have not provided facts suggesting that this economic interest is
implicated, we do not further discuss this basis for disqualification.
4- Are Councilman Madrigal's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
A person, including a source of incomg is directly involved in a govemmental
decision if the person initiates a proceeding by filing an application, claiir, appeal or
similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject oftt " 
proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation t}lO+.1@)(l)-(a)(t).) A
person is the subject of a proceeding concerning the decision before the agency iftt i 
-
"decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial orrevocatiol of *y licensg
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with," that person. (Regulation
l87A4.l(a)(2).) If a person who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, the person is deerned to be indirectly involved.
SEru DIRECTLY INVOLVED
Renegotiations of MOU between the city and SEIU:
SEru is a named party in the renegotiations of the MOU between the city and
SEru. Furthermore, these memoranda represent conkacfual commitments between the
city and SEIU' Thus, SEIU is directly involved in the governmental decisions of the city
council concerning these renegotiations.
SEru INDIRECTLY INVOLVED
Renegotiations of MOU - Operating Engineers:
SEru is not a named party in negotiations of the MOU between the city and
Operating Engineers.
E liminating Yac ant P os i ti ons :
We assume that eliminating vacant employment positions is a matter initiated by
the city council and not the unions (SEIU and,/or Operating Engineers). Thus, the unions
are not the subject of the proceedings in which these decisions will be made and they
would be indirectly involved in this type of decision.
Layoffs of SEIU employees:
The local's appearance in matters involving employee layoffs presumably could
be in a representative capacity rather than being a named party. Wtr." acting in a
representative capacity, we have advised that aperson (which in this instanci would
include the local) is indirectly involved in the govemmental decisions concemin gthat
matter. (Orlik Advice Letter, No. I-98-175; Doyle Advice Letter, No. 4-97-071; Nelson
Advice Letter, No. I-91-443.)
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standmds apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending o, tjr"
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirect! involved
in the agency's decision.
Directly Involved Nonprofit Entities :
When a nonprofit entity that is a source of income to a public official is directly
involved in a governmental decision, should that decision have any financial effect atLn,
evenapenn%onthat entity,itisdeemedtobematerial. (Regulation 18705.3(a).) This
would apply with respect to the SEru MOU.
Indir e ctly Inv o lv e d No np r ofi t Entiti es :3
In all the other decisions you have described, SEIU is indirectly involved.
Regulation 18705.30)(2) lists the materiality standards for source of incomg nonprofit
orgarizations that are indirectly involved in a decision. The thresholds in the regulation
vary depending upon the gross annual receipts of the nonprofit organization. We cannot
determine which materiality standard in the regulation is relevantio your facts because
we do not know SEIU's gross annual receipts. As discussed below, Lo*"r"r, this step
may not be necessary to complete the analysis.
"Nexus Rule":
There is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in cases where
there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source of income ard to the official,s public
agency. The "nexus" rule is only relevant when a decision indirectly affects an economic
interest of the official. Under the "nexus ruIe,,,
"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a
source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public
3 Note that if either the "nexus rule" applies, or if the decision is "inextricably linked,, to the SEIU decisioq
the stricter "one-penny rule" would apply to the decision. (See discussion below.)
official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
(Regulation I 8705.3(c), emphasis added.)
The rationale for the 'hexus" test is that a public official may not accomplish in
his or her public capacity what the public official is paid to accomplish in his or her
private capacity. When a connection exists between a person's job and his or her role as
a public official, there is a presumption that the value of the anployee's services to the
employer is based on the fact that the employee is a public official. Thus, the materiality
threshold is understandably much lower.
In this case, we would look to see when Mr. Madrigal is acting in his official
capacity as a council member, whether he is accomplishing the purpose for which he was
hired by SEIU. However, this is a fact-sensitive question and would need additional facts
concerning his employment with SEru to make a deterrnination.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon Councilmember Madrigalts economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? @oes a conflict of interest result?)
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Renegotiation of MOU -- SEIU:
The dues paid by metnbers of SEIU are income to the union which is determined
according to a formula based on the percentage of mernbers' gross base pay. Any
renegotiation ofthe bargaining agreements that affects the amount of gross wages paid by
the city to SEIU mernbers will have an effect upon the income received by the union.
Since SEru is directly involved in these renegotiations and is subject to the 'bne penny''
rule under regulation 18705.3(a), it is reasonably foreseeable under these facts that the
renegotiation of the bargaining agreements will have a material financial effect on the
union. For this reason, Mr. Madrigal has a conflict of interest prohibiting his
involvement in the city council's renegotiations with SEIU, unless an exception applies.
Renegotiation of MOU - Operating Engineers:
Although the city operates under a separate MOU in connection with Operating
Engineers, many of the provisions of the Operating Engineers MOU are legally required
to be identical to provisions of the SEIU MOU. In effect, negotiations involving certain
provisions of the Operating Engineers MOU would result in identical benefits for SEIU
members. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable under the facts you provide that
renegotiations of the Operating Engineers MOU would have a financial effect on SEIU.
If the effect is material, Councilman Madrigal would have a conflict of interest


Page No. ll
prohibiting his involvement in the city council's renegotiations with Operating Engineers,
unless an exception applies.
E liminating Yac ant Pos i ti ons :
Assuming that elimination of vacant positions is a matter initiated by the city
council and not the unions, SEru would be indirectly involved. Thus it would be sudect
to thresholds in regulation 18705.3(b)Q) which vary depending upon the gross annual
receipts of a nonprofit organization. Because we do not have specific information about
the financial impact of these decisions or know SEIU's gross annual receipts, we cannot
determine ifthe financial effect of the govemmental decision will meet the applicable
materiality standard.
7- and 8. The *Public Generally" Exception and Legally Required participation.
The conflict of interest which is the focus of your question derives from the
financial effect of renegotiations and other decisions involving a source of income to
Councilmember Madrigal. For the "public generallt'' exception to apply,a significant
segment of the public would have to be affected by the decisions in suUitantially the
same manner as the source of income. (Regulation 18707(b).) Since a significant
segment of the public would not receive the same financial benefits that S-BIU will
receive under renegotiation of the MOU with the city council, the "public generally',
exception does not apply.
Section 87101permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
goveflrmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. The rule does not apply when there is an alternatirr".or."" oidecision-
making consistent with the statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708.) Thus, it
only applies when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made without the
participation of the disqualified official. Since you have not provided facts relating to the
application of this exception, we cannot advise you regarding the application.
Segmentation:
Where a decision in which an ofEcial has a conflict of interest is "inextricably
interrelated" to another decision, the official will be required to disqualiff from both
decisions. Regulation 1S709(b) states that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated,
when the result of one decision will effectively determing affirm, nullifu, or alter the
result of another decision."
You suggest that several of the decisions in question which only indirectly
involve SEru may be "inexkicably interrelated" to the decisions in which SEIU is
directly involved. Each of the governmental decisions should be analyzed independently
to determine whether there will be a foreseeable and material financiai effect on the
public official's economic interests. (In re owen (1976) 2 FppC ops.77.) This is
ultimately a factual determination we cannot make.
If the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" to the SEIU MOU decision,
Councilmernber Madrigal maybe able to particrpate in some of these decisions. If the
decisions are "segmented" into a series of decisions, Mr. Madrigal may participate in
those decisions that do not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
his economic interests.
Segmentation may apply as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:
(l) The decision in which the oflicial has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inexkicably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifuing financial interest;
(2) 'fhe decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official,s
" participation in any way; and
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was
disqualified."
Several of your questions related to segmentation.
1. operating Engineers' contracts, adjustment of department head salaries,
and elimination of vacant city employmentposifions:
Applying this regulation to these decisions, the city would first have to make a
decision concerning the SEIU contract without the council member's participation,
regarding both the terms of the contract and elimination of vacant SEIU decisions.
Second, these decisions would have to be final and no subsequent decision could affect
them. Under such circumstances, it appears that the council member may be able to vote
on the Operating Engineers' contracts, the vacant operating engineer positions and
department head salaries. However, whether regulation requirements can be met is a
factual question. Because we do not have specific facts, it is not possible to determine if
a particular decision is subject to segmentation.
2. Budgetary decisions impacting employee salaries and benefits:
Your question concerns budgetary decisions that have an impact on the amount of
city funds available for city operations, including employee salaries and benefits.
You have indicated that in fiscal year ?004t 29.5 percent of all city operating
funds, including general and enterprise operating funds, iere dedicated to sEru
employee compensation and benefits, Jvhile 46.i percent of General rund operating
expenditures were attributable to SEIU employee compensation and benefits.
Thus if the amount of general and enterprise funds increase, so would the amount
available to fund SEIU compensation and benefits. If the amount of general or enterprise
funds decrease, the funds available for SEIU salaries and benefits would also decrease.
However, an increase or decrease in these funds does not necessarily mean a
corresponding increase or decrease in SEIU compensation and benefits. There appears to
be some relation, but without more specific facts, it is not possible to determine if a
particular decision is subject to segmentation. In any "r"ot, 
pursuant to regulation
18709(c), Councilmember Madrigal may participate in afinil vote to udofi or reject the
agency's budget.a

QUESTION: A. Decisions Regarding Councilmember Madrigalrs Employer
1. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during
which the city council is providing direction to its labor negotiators conceming a new
Mou between the city and Service Employees International union 415 ("sEru,)?
a) If he may not participate in the decision, what are his duties when the item is
considered in open session?
b) If the item is heard in closed session, may the council mernber remain in the
room when it is being considered?
c) May he review notes and minutes recorded by council members and city staff
and negotiators during the closed session?
2. May he participate in decisions conceming the layoff of SEIU employees?
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 1 09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
fi;-;i
B. Sesmentation Ouestions
3. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during
which the city council is providing direction to its labornegotiators conceming a new
MOU between the city and Operating Engineers Local No. 3 ("Operating Engineers,), or
subsequent closed sessions concerning implementation, application or interpretation of
that new MOU?
4. May Councilmember Madrigalparticrpate in city council decisions conceming
the elimination of vacant Operating Engineers bargaining unit positions or in decisions
concerning the layoff of Operating Engineers employees?
5. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions in which the
council provides direction to the city manager concerning adjustments of deparknent
head salaries?
6. May Councilmernber Madrigal participate in city council budgetary decisions?
C. Exceptions
7. If Councilmernber Madrigal is disqualified from participating in the above
decisions and deliberations, may he appear before the city council personally, or as part
of a public dernonstration to urge or petition the city council to take a particular action or
make a particular decision relative to the matters under deliberation ana discussion by the
council?
8. May Councilmember Madrigal attend meetings of the SEIU membership or
the SEIU barguningteam at which citylSEIU MOU negotiations will be discusseaZ Uuy
he attend meetings at which the union members will vote to adopt or reject a proposed
SEIU/Union MOU?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Mr. Madrigal will have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him from
participating in city council sessions conceming a new MOU between the city and SEIU,
unless these decisions will have no financial effect at all upon SEru. The prohibition
would apply to both open and closed sessions.
a. Councilmember Madrigal must comply with steps outlined in regulation
18702.5(a)-(d). In an open session meeting, Councilmember Madrigal must publicly
identify the tlpe of economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest, *d th",
he must recuse himself and leave the room. There are special rules for closed session
meetings outlined in regulation 18702.5(c).
b. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifuing conflict of interest,
therefore he cannot rernain in the room. See discussion below.
c. No. Councilmember Madrigal has a disqualifying conflict of interest which
bars him from reviewing non-public information.
2. Because SEIU would be indirectly involved in these decisions,
Councilmember Madrigal's ability to participate in these decisions would depend on their
financial impact on SEIU's gross annual receipts. We me not the finder of fact, thus we
must leave this determination to you and the council mernber pursuant to regulation
t87os.3(b)Q).
3,4 and,5. City Council decisions regarding MOU negotiations between the City
and Operating Engineers, elimination of vacant Operating Engineers positions, layoffs of
Operating Engineers members and adiustments of departrnent head salaries are decisions
thatmay be interlinked with decisions in which SEId, Councilmember Madrigal,s
employer, is directly involved. Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of
interest is "inextricably interrelated" to another decision, the official will be required to
disqualifu from both decisions. Because we are not the finder of fact, we cannot
determine whether thi:se decisions are inextricably interrelated.
6. Councilmember Madrigal may participate in afinal vote to adopt or reject the
agency's budget pursuant to regulation 18709(c). However, without more specific facts
we cannot determine whether Mr. Madri galmay participate in decisions regarding
individual components of the budget. See the segmentation discussion below.
7. Councilmember Madrigal maynot appear before the city council personally to
urge the city council to take a particular action or make aparticular decision unless he is
solely representing his own personal interests. See discussion below.
8. Councilmember Madrigal may attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the
SEru bargaining team as long as he is not making, participating in making or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a goverrmental decision.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 11, 2005 John G. Barisone, City Attorney City of Santa Cruz 333 Church Street Santa Cruz, CA 95060 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-04-271 Dear Mr. Barisone: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Councilmember Tony Madrigal for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS A. Decisions Regarding Councilmember Madrigal's Employer 1. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during which the city council is providing direction to its labor negotiators concerning a new MOU between the city and Service Employees International Union 415 ("SEIU")? a) If he may not participate in the decision, what are his duties when the item is considered in open session? b) If the item is heard in closed session, may the council member remain in the room when it is being considered? c) May he review notes and minutes recorded by council members and city staff and negotiators during the closed session? 2. May he participate in decisions concerning the layoff of SEIU employees? ___________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-04-271 Page No. 2 B. Segmentation Questions 3. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions during which the city council is providing direction to its labor negotiators concerning a new MOU between the city and Operating Engineers Local No. 3 ("Operating Engineers"), or subsequent closed sessions concerning implementation, application or interpretation of that new MOU? 4. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council decisions concerning the elimination of vacant Operating Engineers bargaining unit positions or in decisions concerning the layoff of Operating Engineers employees? 5. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council sessions in which the council provides direction to the city manager concerning adjustments of department head salaries? 6. May Councilmember Madrigal participate in city council budgetary decisions? C. Exceptions 7. If Councilmember Madrigal is disqualified from participating in the above decisions and deliberations, may he appear before the city council personally, or as part of a public demonstration to urge or petition the city council to take a particular action or make a particular decision relative to the matters under deliberation and discussion by the council? 8. May Councilmember Madrigal attend meetings of the SEIU membership or the SEIU bargaining team at which city/SEIU MOU negotiations will be discussed? May he attend meetings at which the union members will vote to adopt or reject a proposed SEIU/Union MOU? CONCLUSIONS 1. No. Mr. Madrigal will have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from participating in city council sessions concerning a new MOU between the city and SEIU, unless these decisions will have no financial effect at all upon SEIU. The prohibition would apply to both open and closed sessions. a. Councilmember Madrigal must comply with steps outlined
